title: Curvature-induced nonlinear anomalous Hall effect in thin magnetic shells
authors: Maria Teresa Mercaldo, Mario Cuoco, Carmine Ortix
abstract: Optoelectronic and nonlinear transport experiments probe the quantum geometric tensor of Bloch states, whose real and imaginary components -- the quantum metric and the Berry curvature -- are typically constrained by symmetry. Here, we show that geometric bending provides a route to engineer such responses in centrosymmetric ferromagnets. Curvature-induced strain gradients across the shell thickness break inversion symmetry and activate an orbital Rashba coupling. In the presence of in-plane magnetization and spin-orbit coupling, this generates spin textures with a nontrivial quantum geometry, leading to an intrinsic nonlinear anomalous Hall effect (NAHE) governed by the quantum metric and maximized when the magnetization aligns with the applied electric field. When geometric deformations further break twofold rotational symmetry around the out-of-plane axis, an additional NAHE emerges, maximal for magnetization perpendicular to the driving electric field and governed by the Berry curvature dipole, thus giving access to the imaginary component of the quantum geometric tensor. These results establish curved ferromagnetic shells as a platform for engineering anisotropic nonlinear transport and for selectively probing both components of the quantum geometric tensor.
summary: This paper shows that the physical curvature of magnetic shells can be used to engineer nonlinear transport properties. By manipulating the geometry, one can selectively access the real and imaginary components of the quantum geometric tensor. This provides a new platform for studying the interplay between geometry and topology in condensed matter systems.
main_problem: The paper investigates how geometric curvature and strain gradients in thin magnetic shells can be used to engineer and probe the quantum geometric tensor (quantum metric and Berry curvature) in centrosymmetric ferromagnets.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that curvature-induced orbital Rashba coupling and broken symmetries in magnetic shells generate an intrinsic nonlinear anomalous Hall effect (NAHE) that allows for the selective probing of both the quantum metric and the Berry curvature dipole.
method: The study uses a theoretical framework to analyze how geometric bending and strain gradients across shell thickness break inversion and rotational symmetries, leading to specific spin textures and transport responses.
model_or_system: The system consists of thin, curved ferromagnetic shells characterized by in-plane magnetization, spin-orbit coupling, and curvature-induced strain gradients.
key_observables: Nonlinear anomalous Hall effect (NAHE), quantum metric, Berry curvature dipole, and spin textures.
important_parameters: In-plane magnetization direction, applied electric field direction, and geometric deformations (curvature and strain gradients).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes the presence of spin-orbit coupling and that geometric bending induces sufficient strain gradients to break inversion symmetry.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of probing the quantum geometric tensor, describes the mechanism of curvature-induced symmetry breaking and orbital Rashba coupling, analyzes the resulting NAHE components, and concludes with the implications for engineering anisotropic nonlinear transport.
